cold_watch.immediate_felt_reaction: The ad opens like a blunt creator confession: editing feels like a chore, and this app is the shortcut.
cold_watch.moment_of_curiosity: The switch from the creator's room to the pink-backed phone UI makes the viewer wait to see whether the shortcut is real.
cold_watch.moment_of_confusion_or_boredom: The final return to the creator is less specific than the product-demo middle, so the action step becomes implicit.
cold_watch.most_memorable_image: A bold 'HOW I SAVE TIME EDITING' headline above a creator, followed by a phone screen showing AI Edit and template controls.

evidence_timeline.observation: Creator appears in a vertical bedroom/studio shot under a large 'HOW I SAVE TIME EDITING' headline with 'with the Captions App' beside it; captions build toward 'You're lazy.'
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The viewer is put inside a familiar guilt loop: wanting output without wanting to do the editing labor.

evidence_timeline.observation: The same creator-facing shot continues while the visible caption asks about editing one's own videos and later shows the word 'experienced.'
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The problem is narrowed from general editing fatigue to the insecurity that editing requires skill or effort the viewer does not want to spend.

evidence_timeline.observation: The ad cuts to a phone UI on a bright pink background: first a clip picker, then a new-video screen with Manual edit, AI Edit, Captions, and Create project controls.
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The shortcut becomes concrete because the app flow starts from an ordinary raw clip rather than an abstract feature list.

evidence_timeline.observation: The browse screen shows style/template cards with categories such as New, Basic, Hollywood, Business, and Trending, plus a 'Select Paper II' button.
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The app is positioned as taste and format selection, not only caption transcription.

evidence_timeline.observation: The editor view shows a preview, timeline thumbnails, Export, a Clip style control, an AI Edit - Talking Head layer, and editing actions such as Add, Curve, Delete, and Delete All.
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The viewer sees the promise turn into an editable artifact, which makes the AI shortcut feel controllable instead of magical.

evidence_timeline.observation: The ad returns from the phone UI to the creator in the room, with short center captions such as 'go back' and 'me.'
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The human creator presence reappears, but the proof density drops because the screen no longer shows a product state.

evidence_timeline.observation: The creator points downward while 'Captions' appears large across his shirt area as a branded end cue.
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The viewer gets a simple brand memory cue, but the next action is less explicit than the product demonstration.

core_mechanism.name: lazy-editor shortcut proof
core_mechanism.why_it_catches_attention: It uses a provocative self-accusation around laziness, then immediately names the desired outcome: saving editing time.
core_mechanism.why_it_might_persuade: The middle shows a raw-clip-to-AI-edit workflow, templates, and timeline controls, so the promise is backed by visible interface proof.
core_mechanism.what_cannot_be_copied: Do not copy the competitor's exact headline, creator likeness, room setup, pink product-demo framing, app UI, captions, music, or brand cues.

creative_intent.creative_label: Captions App Lazy Editor Workflow Shortcut UGC
creative_intent.creative_type: Creator UGC / app walkthrough / workflow-shortcut direct response
creative_intent.likely_hypothesis: If creators feel called out for avoiding edits and then see AI Edit plus templates, they may believe the app removes enough labor to try it.
creative_intent.target_audience: Short-form creators and social teams who have raw footage but resist repetitive manual editing.
creative_intent.funnel_stage: Cold prospecting with app-trial or install intent.
creative_intent.likely_kpi: Click-through, app install, trial start, signup, or project creation.
creative_intent.offer_strategy: Turn editing avoidance into a quick app workflow proof, then leave the brand cue as the action bridge.
creative_intent.marketer_job_to_be_done: Make AI editing feel easy, creator-native, and less intimidating than manual timeline work.
unknowns: No spend, retention, click-through, install, or conversion metrics are available.
The actual targeting and campaign objective are not visible.
The audio transcript was not generated; analysis relies on visible captions and keyframes.

distilled_core.core_thesis: The creative makes editing avoidance feel normal, then resolves it with visible AI-edit workflow proof inside the app.
distilled_core.mechanism_formula: lazy-callout hook -> editing-effort pain -> raw-clip app flow -> style/editor proof -> branded cue
distilled_core.belief_shift_summary: The viewer moves from 'editing my videos is annoying and skill-heavy' to 'I can start with raw clips and let the app do most of it.'
distilled_core.why_it_works: It pairs identity-level friction with concrete UI proof, so the viewer gets both emotional permission and a visible shortcut.
distilled_core.where_it_breaks: The ending is weaker than the middle because the CTA is mostly a brand cue rather than an explicit next step or finished-output reveal.
distilled_core.submagic_transfer: For Submagic, translate the tactic into a raw-clip-to-polished-output proof showing AI Captions, Auto Edit, Magic Clips, B-roll, and Brand Kit controls.
steal: Open with the creator's hidden editing avoidance, not a generic AI feature claim.
Show the workflow path from raw clip to editable output.
Make style selection feel fast and creator-native.
avoid: Do not copy the lazy wording, the Captions App UI, the creator, or the branded visual system.
Do not end with only a soft brand cue if the product proof is the strongest asset.
test_next: Test an explicit finished-video before-after reveal before the end cue.
Test a clearer Submagic CTA tied to a first project or template outcome.

core_understanding.human_truth: Creators often want the status of posting consistently but feel embarrassed by how much editing labor they avoid.

belief_shift.before: Editing my own clips takes time, skill, and patience I may not have.
belief_shift.after: A guided AI workflow can turn my raw footage into an editable short without starting from scratch.
belief_shift.bridge: The ad moves from a guilt-based creator hook to specific product screens: clip picker, AI Edit, templates, timeline, and export.

tension_stack: The creator wants output volume but resents repetitive editing labor.
The creator wants the result to look intentional, not like a raw upload.
The creator distrusts vague AI promises unless the actual app path is visible.
The creator needs to feel in control after AI automation, which the timeline and editing controls partially solve.

proof_ladder.weakness: The first seconds name the benefit but do not yet show the finished result.

proof_ladder.claim: The workflow can start from raw clips.
proof_ladder.proof_type: clip picker and selected talking-head clip
proof_ladder.weakness: The selected clip context is visible, but the ad does not quantify the time saved.

proof_ladder.claim: AI can replace manual edit setup.
proof_ladder.proof_type: AI Edit option and captions toggle
proof_ladder.weakness: The UI implies automation, but the exact AI choices are not fully explained.

proof_ladder.claim: The output can match a chosen style.
proof_ladder.proof_type: template browse screen and editor layers
proof_ladder.weakness: The template options are visible, but the final exported comparison is not clearly shown.

viewer_state_timeline.trigger: A bold time-saving headline plus a lazy-callout caption makes editing avoidance personal.
The caption sequence frames the viewer as someone who does not want to edit their own videos.
The product screen shows raw clip selection, AI Edit, captions, and template browsing.
The editor timeline and layers show that the automated result can still be adjusted.
The creator points down as the Captions brand appears as the final memory cue.

core_understanding.skeptic_read: A skeptical creator may accept that the app has AI-edit controls but still wonder whether the final output is actually better than a simple captioned clip.
core_understanding.transfer_principle_for_submagic: Use a creator's avoided editing task as the hook, then prove the shortcut with an in-product path from raw clip to polished, editable, branded output.
counterfactuals: A first-three-seconds before-after preview could make the time-saving claim feel more concrete.
A stronger final CTA could convert the product proof into a clearer next action.
Removing the product UI middle would leave only a creator claim and significantly weaken believability.

scores.improvement_note: The hook is bold and benefit-led; the main improvement is showing the finished output sooner.
The editing-avoidance pain is clear, but some caption text is only partially captured by OCR.
The UI proof is strong; add a clearer exported before-after moment for extra conviction.
The shift from manual editing to AI-assisted editing is visible, but time saved is not quantified.
The brand cue is visible, but the viewer is not shown a precise next action.
The mechanism maps cleanly to Submagic workflows if surface execution and competitor UI are not copied.

tiktok.notes: Native creator framing, direct-to-camera energy, and fast app-screen proof fit TikTok creative conventions.

reels.notes: The hook and product demo fit Reels, but the large top headline and small UI text need safe-zone review.

shorts.notes: The benefit is clear, though YouTube Shorts may need an earlier before-after payoff to hold broad viewers.

meta_paid.notes: The workflow proof is useful for prospecting, but the CTA and offer are softer than typical direct-response Meta ads.

linkedin.notes: The casual lazy-callout style is less aligned with business buyers unless reframed around agency throughput.

drop_off_hypotheses: Viewers who do not identify with 'lazy editor' humor may drop before the product demo.
The app-screen section could lose viewers on smaller displays if UI text becomes too hard to read.
The final creator-only beat may underperform the product-demo middle because it gives less new information.
risks_and_unknowns: No performance metrics are available, so all judgments are creative-signal inferences only.
OCR is imperfect and no full audio transcript was produced; visible keyframes and manual visual notes carry the analysis.
Sampling may miss intermediate micro-beats between keyframes.
Submagic adaptations should borrow the mechanism, not the competitor UI, creator likeness, wording, colors, or brand cues.
